menu_title: Explain It Like I Am the CFO -- Defending a Technical Decision
menu_desc: Kill the jargon: plain words, one analogy, the business benefit
title_line: Explain It Like I Am the
title_sub: Defending a Technical Decision to People Who Do Not Speak Technology
ch5_sub: She does not care about the database. She cares about the money.
ch6_sub: Quick fire, three role-plays, and an executive with sixty seconds

plan_row.tema: Explain It Like I Am the CFO -- Defending a Technical Decision
plan_row.foco: which means + comparatives (cheaper / faster than) + analogy (it is like...)
plan_row.atividade: One-slide answer + role-play with an executive who hates jargon
plan_row.homework: Record a 90-sec explanation of a technical decision, with zero jargon

heading: Sixty Seconds, No
Jargon
warmup.intro: Last class you raised a risk, and you ended like this:

callback.text: I need one decision from you by Friday.
callback.speak: I need one decision from you by Friday.

warmup.box_title: Today they give you sixty seconds -- and they do not speak your language.
warmup.box_text: The steering committee wants to know why you are spending four weeks replacing a database that "already works". Explain it. No acronyms.

goal.d: Three role-plays, and I interrupt you

heading: The Language of Plain
English
vocab_ch.sub: 8 words to explain technology to people who pay for it

vocab.def: Technical words that only your team understands
vocab.def_pc: technical words that only your team understands
vocab.ex: No jargon, please: they are not engineers.

vocab.def: A comparison with something everybody knows
vocab.def_pc: a comparison with something everybody knows
vocab.hint: A comparison with something everybody knows
vocab.ex: Let me give you an analogy: it is like an old car.

vocab.def: To give good reasons for a decision
vocab.def_pc: to give good reasons for a decision
vocab.hint: To give good reasons for a decision
vocab.ex: I can justify the four weeks in one sentence.

vocab.def: What the company gains -- in money, time or safety
vocab.def_pc: what the company gains: money, time or safety
vocab.hint: What the company gains from it
vocab.ex: The benefit is four hours saved every week.

vocab.def: Worth the money you pay for it
vocab.def_pc: worth the money you pay for it
vocab.ex: It is cheaper to maintain, so it is cost-effective.

vocab.def: To keep a system working, month after month
vocab.def_pc: to keep a system working, month after month
vocab.hint: To keep it working, month after month
vocab.ex: The old system is expensive to maintain.

vocab.def: Old technology that still runs the business
vocab.def_pc: old technology that still runs the business
vocab.hint: Old technology that still runs the business
vocab.ex: The legacy database is fifteen years old.

vocab.def: How many moving parts a system has
vocab.def_pc: how many moving parts a system has

heading: A Decision Nobody
Understood
vocab_check.prompt: Think of a technical decision at Oracle that the business side never understood.
vocab_check.sub: Talk for 90 seconds: what did you decide, how did you explain it, and where did you lose them?

reading.ch_sub: A short text about why smart engineers lose the room
heading: Nobody Buys the
Architecture
paras: Engineers lose executives in the first ten seconds, and they never know why. They start with the technology: the database, the containers, the pipeline. The executive hears three words she does not know, decides that this is not her problem, and opens her laptop.

The fix is not to speak more slowly. It is to change what you start with. Start with the money, the time or the risk -- what the company gains, or what it loses. The technology is the reason, and the reason comes second, in one short sentence.

Then, if you need one more level, use an analogy. 'The legacy database is like a car from 2010 that we keep repairing: every repair is cheap, and we do six a year.' Nobody buys the architecture. They buy the result, and the result has to be in their language.

choices: Executives are not intelligent enough to understand technical details.
Start with the business result, then give the technical reason in one sentence -- and use an analogy.
You should explain the architecture slowly, with more detail.

tf: You should open with the technology, so people understand the decision.
Open with the money, the time or the risk. The technology comes second.
An analogy helps when the topic is technical.
'It is like a car from 2010 that we keep repairing.'
Executives buy the result, not the architecture.
The result has to be in their language -- money, time, risk.

heading: How to Explain It in Sixty
Seconds
bank.sub: The result → the reason → the analogy → the cost of doing nothing → the ask
bank.label: The five lines of a plain-English answer
items: In plain terms: we spend four hours every week fixing the old database.
We are replacing it, which means those four hours go back to the client.
The legacy system is like a car from 2010: every repair is cheap, and we do six a year.
The new one is cheaper to maintain, and safer for the client's data.
If we do nothing, the cost is the same four hours, every week, forever.
I need four weeks. After that, the team is faster, and the risk is lower.

grammar.prompt: Which one translates? Which one compares? Which one makes you see it?

discovery.html: "We are replacing the database, which means the system does not stop on Mondays."
discovery.speak: We are replacing the database, which means the system does not stop on Mondays.

discovery.html: "The new system is cheaper to maintain than the old one."
discovery.speak: The new system is cheaper to maintain than the old one.

discovery.html: "The legacy database is like a car from 2010."
discovery.speak: The legacy database is like a car from 2010.

discovery.html: "In plain terms, we lose four hours every week."
discovery.speak: In plain terms, we lose four hours every week.

rows: We are replacing the database, which means the system does not stop.
The new system is cheaper to maintain than the old one.
The legacy database is like a car from 2010.
grammar.note: Start with the result, never with the technology. The executive is not buying a database -- she is buying four hours a week, a lower risk, and a bill she can defend. Say that first. The technology is one sentence, and it comes second.
mistakes: The new system is more cheaper to maintain.
The new system is cheaper to maintain.
We chose it because is faster and more safe.
We chose it because it is faster and safer.
grammar.mistake_note: Adjetivos curtos ganham -er (cheap → cheaper, safe → safer) -- nunca more + -er juntos. E toda oração em inglês precisa de sujeito: because it is faster.

heading: The One-Slide
Answer
artefact.title: Four Weeks. Here Is Why.
artefact.reason: In plain terms: we spend four hours every week keeping a fifteen-year-old system alive
rows: Benefit: those four hours go back to client work, every week
Analogy: it is like a car from 2010 -- six cheap repairs a year
Risk: the legacy database stores client data, and it is out of support
Cost of doing nothing: the same four hours a week, forever
artefact.footer: One slide. Sixty seconds. Zero acronyms. If they want the architecture, they will ask.

heading: Sixty Seconds, and No
Acronyms
listenings.text: Diogo, you are on the agenda for the steering committee at three. You have sixty seconds, and I want to warn you about this room. The CFO is there. She does not know what a container is, and she does not want to know. Last quarter an architect used the word Kubernetes twice and she stopped listening after the first one. So do not open with the technology. Open with money or time. Tell her what we lose today, and what we get back. If you need to explain how it works, give her an analogy, something from real life. And whatever you do, do not say the word migration. Say what changes for the client.
comp: Sixty seconds, in the steering committee at three.
Money or time -- what the company loses today, and what it gets back.
What should he use if he has to explain how it works?
An analogy, something from real life.

heading: The Explanation,
Done Well
listenings.lead: This is the target. Listen, then do it your way.
listenings.text: Thank you. In plain terms: every week, my team spends four hours keeping a fifteen-year-old system alive. Four hours that the client pays for and does not see. We are replacing that system, which means those four hours go back to client work, every week, from July. Here is an analogy. The legacy database is like a car from 2010. Every repair is cheap, so we keep repairing it -- six times a year. At some point you are not saving money, you are just spending it slowly. The new system is cheaper to maintain, and it is safer, because the old one is out of support and it stores client data. If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours a week, forever. I need four weeks and no new features in that time. After that, the team is faster, and the risk is lower.
comp: Time and money: four hours a week keeping an old system alive.
A car from 2010 with six cheap repairs a year.
Four weeks, and no new features during that time.

heading: I Do Not Care About the
Database

lines.html: Diogo, sixty seconds. Why are we spending four weeks on a system that already works?
lines.speak: Diogo, sixty seconds. Why are we spending four weeks on a system that already works?

lines.html: In plain terms: we spend four hours every week keeping it alive. That is a whole morning, every week.
lines.speak: In plain terms: we spend four hours every week keeping it alive. That is a whole morning, every week.

lines.html: Four hours is not four weeks. Justify the four weeks.
lines.speak: Four hours is not four weeks. Justify the four weeks.

lines.html: The legacy database is like a car from 2010. Every repair is cheap, so we keep repairing -- six times a year.
lines.speak: The legacy database is like a car from 2010. Every repair is cheap, so we keep repairing. Six times a year.

lines.html: And the new one? Do not give me jargon.
lines.speak: And the new one? Do not give me jargon.

lines.html: It is cheaper to maintain, and safer, which means the client's data is not in a system that nobody supports.
lines.speak: It is cheaper to maintain, and safer, which means the client's data is not in a system that nobody supports.

lines.html: The client will ask why they pay for four weeks of nothing.
lines.speak: The client will ask why they pay for four weeks of nothing.

lines.html: They do not pay for nothing. They pay four hours a week today, forever. The benefit starts in July.
lines.speak: They do not pay for nothing. They pay four hours a week today, forever. The benefit starts in July.

lines.html: Now that I can defend. Send me that in one slide -- with the analogy.
lines.speak: Now that I can defend. Send me that in one slide, with the analogy.

comp: What does Sophia ask him to justify?
The four weeks -- not the four hours.
What does she not want to hear?
What does she ask for at the end?

quickfire.situation: Open the answer with money or time, in one sentence.
tips: 'In plain terms, we lose four hours a week.'
No technology in the first sentence.

quickfire.situation: Explain the technical reason -- in one sentence, no acronyms.
tips: If you use a technical word, translate it immediately.

quickfire.situation: Give an analogy from real life.
tips: Cars, houses, kitchens -- things everybody knows.

quickfire.situation: She says: 'The system works. Why touch it?' Answer.
tips: 'The same four hours, every week, forever.'

quickfire.situation: She asks the price. You do not have the exact number.
tips: Give a range and a date for the exact number.

quickfire.situation: Close in one sentence: what you need, and what she gets.
tips: 'I need four weeks. After that, the team is faster.'

items: Explain it again, in one sentence, as if I were the client.
How do I know this is not just engineers who want a new toy?
What happens if I say no today?
pressure.followup: Result first, reason second, analogy third. If she asks for the architecture, she will ask -- do not volunteer it.

heading: Sixty Seconds With the
Committee
guided.prompt: The committee gives you sixty seconds. Defend the four weeks.
guided.sub: Use the one-slide answer. Five moves, in this order:
chips: it is like a car from 2010

heading: The CFO
Interrupts
semi.quote: Stop. I did not understand a word of that.
semi.sub: She interrupts you twice: the first time she says it is too technical, the second time she asks for the number. Start again with money or time, and give her one analogy she can repeat to the client.
chips: the cost of doing nothing is...

heading: Your Real
Decision
free.headline: Ninety seconds. No notes. No jargon.
free.sub: Take a real technical decision from your project at Oracle. Explain it to a person who does not work in technology: the result first, the reason in one sentence, one analogy, and the cost of doing nothing.

survival.en: In plain terms, we lose four hours every week.
survival.pt: Em termos simples, perdemos quatro horas por semana.

survival.en: We are replacing it, which means the system does not stop.
survival.pt: Estamos trocando o sistema, o que significa que ele não para mais.

survival.en: The legacy database is like a car from 2010.
survival.pt: O banco de dados legado é como um carro de 2010.

survival.en: The new one is cheaper to maintain, and safer.
survival.pt: O novo é mais barato de manter, e mais seguro.

survival.en: If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours forever.
survival.pt: Se não fizermos nada, pagamos as mesmas quatro horas para sempre.

checks: I open with money, time or risk -- never with the technology.
I can translate a technical fact with "which means".
I use comparatives correctly: cheaper, faster, safer.
I can explain a system with an analogy from real life.
I can say the cost of doing nothing.

closing.line: You had sixty seconds, they interrupted you twice, and the CFO can now repeat your answer.
closing.next_desc: Chasing a colleague who owes you work -- and who does not report to you

order_audio.text: Sophia gives Diogo sixty seconds and asks why the project needs four weeks. Diogo opens with time: four hours every week keeping the old system alive. She asks him to justify the four weeks, and he gives the analogy of the car from 2010. He explains the benefit: cheaper to maintain, and safer for the client's data. Finally, Sophia asks for the whole answer in one slide.
items: Diogo opens with time: four hours every week on the old system.
He explains the benefit: cheaper to maintain, and safer.
Sophia gives him sixty seconds to justify four weeks.
Sophia asks for the answer in one slide, with the analogy.
He gives the analogy of the car from 2010.

preclass.desc: Explain technology to people who pay for it: result first, reason second, one analogy, no jargon.
preclass.context: The steering committee gives Diogo sixty seconds, and the CFO is in the room. He does not open with the database -- that would be jargon in the first ten seconds. He opens with time: "In plain terms, we lose four hours every week keeping a fifteen-year-old system alive." Then he justifies the four weeks with an analogy: the legacy database is like a car from 2010, with six cheap repairs a year. The technical reason comes in one sentence: the new system is cheaper to maintain and safer, which means the client's data is not sitting in a system nobody supports. That is the benefit, and it is cost-effective. Every extra tool would only add complexity, so he does not mention any. And he closes with the cost of doing nothing: the same four hours, every week, forever.
quiz_ctx: Why does Diogo not open with the database?
Because the executives stop listening at the first technical word.
Because the database is not important.
Because he does not know how it works.
The new system is cheaper to maintain.
The new system is more cheaper to maintain.
The new system is more cheap to maintain.
What is the function of "which means"?
It translates a technical fact into a business result.
It adds a second technical detail.
preclass.tip_sub: Explicar tecnologia para quem paga por ela: resultado primeiro, motivo depois, uma analogia. / Explaining technology to the people who pay for it.
tip_rows: fato técnico + which means + resultado de negócio
We are replacing it, which means the system does not stop.
The new one is cheaper to maintain than the old one.
It is like + algo que todo mundo conhece
The legacy database is like a car from 2010.
preclass.tip_key: Adjetivo curto leva -er (cheap → cheaper, safe → safer): nunca "more cheaper". E toda oração precisa de sujeito: "because it is faster", nunca "because is faster".

blanks.hint: Hint: in plain ___ = sem jargão, em termos simples
blanks.phrase: In plain terms, we lose four hours every week.
blanks.post: , we lose four hours every week."

blanks.hint: Hint: translates the fact into a business result / o que significa que
blanks.phrase: We are replacing it, which means the system does not stop.

blanks.hint: Hint: it is ___ a car from 2010 (analogy) / como
blanks.phrase: The legacy database is like a car from 2010.

blanks.hint: Hint: comparative of cheap -- never "more cheap"
blanks.phrase: The new one is cheaper to maintain, and safer.

blanks.hint: Hint: to keep it working, month after month / manter
blanks.phrase: The old system is expensive to maintain.

blanks.hint: Hint: technical words only your team understands / jargão
blanks.phrase: No jargon, please: they are not engineers.

preclass.speech_intro: The five lines of a plain-English answer. Record each one. Slow and simple -- if she has to ask what a word means, you lost her.
quiz_sit: The CFO asks why the project needs four weeks. You open with:
"In plain terms, we lose four hours every week keeping the old system alive."
"We are migrating from a monolith to a containerized architecture."
"It is very technical, you would not be interested."
She says: "The system works. Why touch it?" The best answer is:
"If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours every week, forever."
"Because the technology is old and ugly."
"Everybody in the market is doing this."
She asks how it works. You:
Give one analogy from real life, in one sentence.
Explain the architecture, layer by layer.
Say it is too complex to explain.
think: Take a real technical decision from your project at Oracle. Speak for 90 seconds to someone who does not work in technology: (1) the result in money or time, (2) the reason, in ONE sentence, (3) an analogy from real life, (4) the cost of doing nothing, (5) what you need. Record it.
Now she interrupts: "Stop. I did not understand a word of that." Start again, out loud, with different words -- and this time do not use a single technical term. Record it.

complementary.title: The Big Short (2015)
complementary.desc: The film stops and explains hard financial words with simple examples. That is exactly what you must do with executives.
complementary.tip: Watch one explanation scene, then explain one technical decision from your work in 60 seconds, with no jargon.

complementary.title: Skills 360 -- Communicating Clearly in English (1)
complementary.desc: This lesson is about clear speaking: simple words, slower speed, and no acronyms or jargon.
complementary.tip: Record yourself explaining a technical topic and check if you used any acronym without explaining it.

complementary.title: How to Explain Technical Concepts to Non-Technical Stakeholders
complementary.desc: A 3-step method to explain software topics to people who are not technical. It focuses on impact, not on details.
complementary.tip: Apply the 3 steps to one decision from your last sprint and say it out loud.

teacher.title: Abertura (2 min): Compartilhe a tela. Diga: 'Last class you raised a risk. Today you have sixty seconds in front of the CFO -- and she does not speak your language.' Avise: hoje ele fala quase o tempo todo.
teacher.warmup: Warm-up falado (4 min): Toque a frase da Aula 13. Depois a virada: 'They do not care about the database. They care about the bill.' Faca a pergunta do box e cale a boca 90 segundos.
teacher.goal: Objetivo (1 min): 'Words, a short text, and then you explain -- and I interrupt you.' Rapido.
teacher.vocab_transition: Transicao vocab (1 min): 'Eight words. Click each card.' Ferramenta, nao aula de vocabulario.
teacher.vocab1: Vocab reveal 1-4 (4 min): Pista, tentativa, reveal, audio, uma frase real. CCQ 'jargon': 'Is "container" jargon for a CFO? (Yes.)' CCQ 'analogy': 'Is an analogy technical or everyday? (Everyday.)' CCQ 'benefit': 'Is "new architecture" a benefit? (No -- four hours saved IS.)'
teacher.vocab2: Vocab reveal 5-8 (4 min): Mesma dinamica. CCQ 'legacy': 'Is legacy technology broken? (No -- old, but still running the business.)' CCQ 'maintain': 'Is maintaining free? (No -- it is four hours a week.)' CCQ 'complexity': 'Does a new tool reduce complexity? (Usually it adds.)'
teacher.vocab_check: Vocab check falado (4 min): 90 segundos sobre uma decisao tecnica que o negocio nunca entendeu. Minimo cinco palavras novas. Voce so escuta e conta.
teacher.ch3: Transicao leitura (1 min): 'Short text. Read it fast -- then tell me the last time you lost a room in the first ten seconds.' A leitura e trampolim.
teacher.reading: Leitura + gist (5 min): 90 segundos de leitura silenciosa. Depois o gist. E entao a pergunta que vale a aula: 'Is this true at Oracle, or do your executives actually want the detail?' Dois minutos de fala dele.
teacher.tf: True / False (3 min): Ele decide em voz alta ANTES de clicar, e justifica com uma frase do texto. Rapido.
teacher.bank: Useful language falado (4 min): Cada frase lida em voz alta e imediatamente reescrita para uma decisao tecnica REAL dele. Seis frases da tela, seis frases dele.
teacher.grammar: Grammar discovery (4 min): Toque as 4 frases. Pergunta unica: 'Which one translates? Which one compares? Which one makes you SEE it?' Guie: which means = traduz; cheaper than = compara; it is like = ilustra.
teacher.rule: Reveal the Rule (3 min): Clique. Traduzir, comparar, ilustrar. Exija 2 frases dele com 'which means' sobre o projeto real. CCQ: 'More cheaper -- correct? (No: cheaper.)'
teacher.mistake: Common Mistake (2 min): 'more cheaper' e 'because is faster' (sujeito faltando). Peca as versoes certas em voz alta, duas vezes.
teacher.ch5: Transicao (1 min): 'The CFO is in the room, and she has sixty seconds for you.' Suba a tensao.
teacher.listening1: Listening 1 (4 min): 'Sophia tells you how this room works -- and which word kills you.' Toque uma vez, sem texto.
teacher.listening1_comp: Comprehension falada (3 min): Respostas em voz alta antes do clique. Depois: 'Why does she say -- do not use the word migration?' Deixe ele deduzir falando.
teacher.artefact: Artefato (3 min): Nao leia por ele. 'Read the one-slide answer, then say it out loud in sixty seconds.' Cronometre de verdade. Se passar de 60s, mande cortar.
teacher.dialogue: Dialogo (6 min): Voce e a Sophia e voce INTERROMPE: 'four hours is not four weeks', 'do not give me jargon'. Antes de cada fala do Diogo, ELE fala primeiro; so entao clique e toque o audio.
teacher.dialogue_comp: Dialogue comprehension (2 min): Perguntas sobre a SOPHIA. Depois: 'Which sentence convinced her?' Deixe ele defender a resposta.
teacher.listening2: Listening 2 (4 min): 'This is the explanation, done well -- 60 seconds, zero jargon.' Toque uma vez. Depois: 'What is the analogy? What is the cost of doing nothing?' -- ele fala.
teacher.ch6: Transicao (1 min): 'From here you explain, and I interrupt. Three times.'
teacher.quickfire: Quick Fire (5 min): Uma situacao por vez, resposta imediata. Sem correcao entre elas -- fluencia primeiro.
teacher.rp_guided: Role-play GUIADO (4 min): Voce e o comite. Ele defende as quatro semanas em 60 segundos, usando o one-slide answer. Cronometre. Se comecar pela tecnologia, pare e mande recomecar.
teacher.rp_semi: Role-play SEMI-LIVRE (5 min): Voce interrompe duas vezes: 'I did not understand a word' e depois 'give me the number'. Ele tem de recomecar pelo dinheiro/tempo e dar uma analogia que VOCE consiga repetir ao cliente.
teacher.rp_free: Role-play LIVRE (5 min): Sem apoio. 'A real technical decision. Ninety seconds. Zero jargon. Go.' NAO interrompa, cronometre e conte quantas palavras tecnicas escaparam.
teacher.pressure: Under pressure (4 min): Tres perguntas duras, uma a uma, sem aviso. Resultado primeiro, motivo depois. Se hesitar mais de 3 segundos, repita a pergunta.
teacher.survival: Survival Card (3 min): 'Five phrases. With these you can explain anything to anyone.' Toque cada uma; ele repete devagar e simples.
teacher.learned: Wrap-up (3 min): Ele clica cada check e diz a frase que prova. So depois devolva as 2 correcoes. Homework ORAL: gravar 90 segundos explicando uma decisao tecnica real, sem uma unica palavra tecnica.
teacher.closing: Fechamento (1 min): Homework ORAL: gravar 90 segundos sem jargao e enviar. Anuncie a Aula 15: 'Next time you chase a colleague who owes you work -- and who does not report to you.'
